food_name: Stir-Fried Beef with Rice (Cơm Bò Lúc Lắc)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Vietnamese shaking beef with rice (Cơm bò lúc lắc). Mapped to the broader 'beef dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Beef meat dish — stir-fried, with rice
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (beef meat dish) used as the closest defensible category. No specific code exists for Vietnamese bò lúc lắc with rice in the FoodEx2 hierarchy; closest_parent match applied.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific Vietnamese dish. SNOMED CT does not provide granular coverage of Southeast Asian prepared dishes.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Cơm bò lúc lắc as served in Vietnamese restaurants typically weighs approximately 550 g, comprising roughly 200–220 g of steamed white rice and 200–220 g of stir-fried marinated beef cubes, with the remainder being fresh vegetables and sauce.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Beef, short loin, top loin steak, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 1/8 inch fat, cooked, broiled; and White Rice, cooked, enriched — composite estimation for mixed dish
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Cơm bò lúc lắc as a composite dish. Nutrient values were estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked beef (lean, stir-fried equivalent) and cooked white rice in approximate proportions consistent with the dish composition (~40% beef, ~40% rice, ~20% vegetables and sauce). The input record's per-100g values (energy 136 kcal, protein 6.9 g, fat 5.5 g, carbohydrate 15.5 g) are consistent with this composite and have been retained with minor rounding. Sodium 327 mg/100g reflects soy sauce and oyster sauce contributions and is plausible for this preparation. FAO/INFOODS SEA table consulted as a secondary cross-check for Southeast Asian rice and beef values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; and University of Sydney GI Database — White rice, boiled (GI ~64–72)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Cơm bò lúc lắc as a composite dish. The GI of 64 is estimated based on the dominant carbohydrate source being white rice (GI ~64 per University of Sydney GI Database for boiled white rice). The beef and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrate and do not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (64 × 85.25) / 100 ≈ 54.6, rounded to 54.3 using 15.5 g/100g × 550 g serving = 85.25 g carbohydrate per plate.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce, oyster sauce, and fish sauce — approximately 327 mg sodium per 100 g, equating to ~1,799 mg sodium per standard 550 g plate, which represents approximately 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium preparation (low-sodium soy sauce, oyster sauce on the side) and limit portion size to no more than half a standard plate to keep sodium intake within safe daily limits.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice dominates the carbohydrate load at 15.5 g/100g with very low dietary fibre (0.6 g/100g), producing a high glycemic load of approximately 54 per standard serving, which is likely to cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation in individuals with type 2 diabetes.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to reduce the glycemic load, reduce the rice portion to no more than half a cup cooked, and increase the proportion of beef and non-starchy vegetables to moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat content of 1.8 g/100g from beef and cooking oil, combined with cholesterol at 54.6 mg/100g and high sodium (~327 mg/100g), contributes to cardiovascular risk through LDL elevation and blood pressure load; a full 550 g serving delivers approximately 9.9 g saturated fat and ~1,799 mg sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to 250–300 g, request lean beef cuts with minimal added oil, and avoid extra sauce to reduce combined saturated fat and sodium intake to cardioprotective levels.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beef is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6 fatty acid), which can amplify pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-6) relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; nightshade vegetables (tomatoes, bell peppers) present in the dish may trigger joint inflammation in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consider substituting beef with chicken or tofu, request removal of nightshade vegetables, and limit soy sauce and oyster sauce to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cơm bò lúc lắc (Vietnamese shaking beef with rice) is a classic Vietnamese dish consisting of tender cubes of marinated beef — typically sirloin or tenderloin — that are rapidly stir-fried at high heat in a wok with garlic, oyster sauce, soy sauce, fish sauce, and a small amount of butter or oil, producing a caramelised, savoury crust while remaining pink and juicy inside. The name 'lúc lắc' (shaking) refers to the characteristic tossing motion used during stir-frying. The beef is served alongside fragrant steamed white rice and accompanied by fresh lettuce, sliced tomatoes, and sometimes pickled onions or a lime-pepper dipping sauce. The dish delivers approximately 136 kcal per 100 g, with 6.9 g protein, 5.5 g fat, and 15.5 g carbohydrate, making a standard 550 g plate approximately 748 kcal. Sodium is notably high at ~327 mg/100g due to the soy and oyster sauce marinade, making this dish a concern for hypertensive individuals. The white rice base creates a high glycemic load (~54 per serving), which is clinically significant for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. The dish is culturally significant in Vietnamese cuisine and is widely served in Vietnamese restaurants across Southeast Asia, including Singapore. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans, and contains soy, gluten, and fish allergens from the marinade components.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Cơm bò lúc lắc as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for cooked beef and white rice in proportions consistent with the dish, cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA values. The input record's per-100g values are internally consistent with this composite and have been retained. GI is estimated from the dominant carbohydrate source (white rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach rather than a direct approved-source measurement.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026. The input record listed duplicate portions (two identical '1 plate / 550 g' entries); only one has been retained. The input record listed 'country_of_origin: Singapore' which likely reflects the data collection context (GrabFood Singapore) rather than the dish's true culinary origin (Vietnam); region_of_origin has been corrected to Vietnam. Halal status is marked false as the dish typically contains oyster sauce and fish sauce whose halal certification cannot be confirmed without vendor-specific information. trans_fat_g of 0.09 g/100g is retained from the input record as a plausible trace value for beef-based stir-fry; this is within the naturally occurring trans-fat range for ruminant beef. FoodEx2 match type corrected from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed enumeration values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'], resolving the G5 hard gate failure from iteration 2.
